item.title: DotZap 1.1.1
item.description:
# DotZap 1.1.1
The first public release of DotZap — a macOS menu-bar utility that silently deletes Apple/Windows metadata junk (`._*`, `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`, `desktop.ini`, …) the moment external drives create them.
## New
### What it does - **Watches every mounted volume** automatically. Plug a drive in and DotZap starts cleaning within a second; eject it and DotZap stops. - **Cleans nine kinds of metadata junk out of the box**: Apple Double `._*`, `.DS_Store`, `.Spotlight-V100`, `.Trashes`, `.fseventsd`, `.TemporaryItems`, `__MACOSX`, `Thumbs.db`, and `desktop.ini`. Each rule is individually toggleable. - **Custom rules**. Add your own patterns — exact name, prefix, or glob — for anything else you'd rather not see (`*.tmp~`, `.obsidian`, you pick). - **Per-volume control**. Disable cleaning on a specific drive without touching the global setting, or use the per-volume **whitelist** to protect files that match a built-in rule but you actually want to keep (e.g. `*.psd` on a Photoshop drive). - **Clean Now**. Tap the button in a volume's expanded row to do a one-shot recursive scan instead of waiting for the next file event. - **Activity log** with lifetime stats. See exactly what was deleted, when, from where, and how much space you've recovered. Capped at 500 recent entries; lifetime totals persist across launches.
### The interface - **Menu-bar only** — no Dock icon, no main window, no floating clutter. The `sparkles.rectangle.stack` icon brightens when active and dims when paused. - **Left-click** the icon to pause or resume globally. - **Right-click** to open a glass-styled settings panel (frosted vibrancy, rounded corners, subtle stroke — built to feel like a first-party utility). Click anywhere outside it to dismiss. - **Three tabs**: Volumes, Rules, Activity. Plus an **About** tab with version info, a manual *Check for Updates…* button, and a Quit shortcut (⌘Q). - **Launch at Login** toggle in the Volumes tab — DotZap starts itself when you sign in, no Login Items dance required.
### Auto-updates - **Built-in Sparkle updates** with EdDSA-signed releases. New versions get a non-blocking prompt within 24 hours; you can disable auto-checks in the About tab and still trigger manual checks. Every update's signature is verified against a public key embedded at build time before installation.
### Smart about what it can't touch - DotZap **silently skips paths inside system-owned directories** like `.TemporaryItems/`, `.Spotlight-V100/`, `.Trashes/`, and `.fseventsd/`. These are root-owned even on external drives, so deletion would always fail. The first attempt on each protected location is remembered for the session — no retry storm, no log spam. - **Full Disk Access banner** in the Volumes tab walks you through granting the required system permission and re-checks after you click Open Settings.
### Polish - **App icon** rendered from the same SF Symbol used in the menu bar — white glyph on a blue gradient squircle, generated at all 10 macOS sizes (16/32/128/256/512 @ 1x and 2x). - **Compact About tab layout** — every detail fits in one view at the panel's default size; no scrolling required.
## License
DotZap is now licensed under the [GNU General Public License v3.0](https://github.com/Foiler25/DotZap/blob/main/LICENSE). You're free to modify and redistribute your own builds, as long as the source is made available under the same license.
## Install & update
- **Already installed?** DotZap auto-updates — pick *Check for Updates…* in the About tab to grab it now, or wait for the next scheduled check. - **First time?** Download `DotZap-1.1.1.dmg`, open it, drag DotZap to Applications. First launch: right-click → **Open**, then System Settings → **Privacy & Security** → **Open Anyway**. Then grant Full Disk Access via the banner in the Volumes tab.
**Requires macOS 14 (Sonoma) or later.**

item.title: DotZap 1.1.2
item.description:
# DotZap 1.1.2
A small but important bugfix and quality-of-life release.
## Fixed
- **Right-click now opens the panel on macOS Tahoe.** On macOS 26, the system silently consumed right-clicks (and Ctrl-Left-Clicks) on menu-bar status icons unless the app assigned an explicit menu. DotZap now intercepts these clicks before the system swallows them, so the glass settings panel opens reliably across macOS 14, 15, and 26.
## Changed
- **Permission prompt and initial cleanup happen automatically on launch.** When DotZap detects a mounted drive at startup (or when you plug one in), it now runs a one-shot scan immediately. The macOS "Removable Volumes" permission prompt appears right away rather than waiting for you to click *Clean Now* — and any junk that accumulated while DotZap wasn't running gets cleaned without you having to do anything. - **Significantly lower CPU and memory under heavy churn.** During bursts of file activity (e.g. a Finder copy or an Xcode build to a watched drive), DotZap previously did filesystem deletions on the main thread and wrote to disk after every single file. It now batches deletions onto a background queue and coalesces persistence writes so the panel stays smooth and idle CPU/RAM stay tiny. - **Drive icons load once per volume.** The Volumes tab no longer re-allocates a new icon image on every UI refresh.
## Install & update
- **Already installed?** DotZap auto-updates — pick *Check for Updates…* in the About tab to grab it now, or wait for the next scheduled check. - **First time?** Download `DotZap-1.1.2.dmg`, open it, drag DotZap to Applications. First launch: right-click → **Open**, then System Settings → **Privacy & Security** → **Open Anyway**. Then grant Full Disk Access via the banner in the Volumes tab.
**Requires macOS 14 (Sonoma) or later.**

item.title: DotZap 1.1.3
item.description:
# DotZap 1.1.3
A small visual polish release.
## Fixed
- **Rules tab spacing.** When the Custom Rules section was empty, the "No custom rules yet." message sat flush against the panel edge instead of aligning with the section header above it. The text now matches the header's indent.
## Install & update
- **Already installed?** DotZap auto-updates — pick *Check for Updates…* in the About tab to grab it now, or wait for the next scheduled check. - **First time?** Download `DotZap-1.1.3.dmg`, open it, drag DotZap to Applications. First launch: right-click → **Open**, then System Settings → **Privacy & Security** → **Open Anyway**. Then grant Full Disk Access via the banner in the Volumes tab.
**Requires macOS 14 (Sonoma) or later.**

item.title: DotZap 1.2.0
item.description:
# DotZap 1.2.0
This release puts you in charge of *how* DotZap deletes. You can now send matched files to the Trash instead of vaporizing them, and set a size cap so large files are protected even when a rule would otherwise sweep them up.
## New
- **Move to Trash (default on).** Matched files now go to the Trash by default, so anything DotZap removes is recoverable from Finder. Toggle it off in the About tab if you'd rather have permanent deletes like before. - **Size-cap safety net.** A new "Skip files larger than" setting (default **50 MB**) holds back any file over the limit, even if a rule matches. Adjust the threshold in the About tab — handy if you want aggressive rules without the risk of nuking a stray disk image or video. - **Skipped files are visible.** When a file is held back by the size cap, it shows up in the Activity log with an orange **SKIPPED** badge and a tooltip explaining which rule matched. Nothing is silently ignored.
## Changed
- **Lifetime stats now count only real deletions.** Files that get skipped by the size cap are logged in Activity but don't inflate your "files deleted" or "bytes freed" totals. - **Accurate version in About.** The About tab now reports the correct app version and build number (previous builds could show a stale `1.0.0 (1)`).
## Install & update
- **Already installed?** DotZap auto-updates — pick *Check for Updates…* in the About tab to grab it now, or wait for the next scheduled check. - **First time?** Download `DotZap-1.2.0.dmg`, open it, drag DotZap to Applications. First launch: right-click → Open, then System Settings → Privacy & Security → Open Anyway. Then grant Full Disk Access via the banner in the Volumes tab.

item.title: DotZap 1.2.1
item.description:
# DotZap 1.2.1
A small but useful follow-up to 1.2.0: try out new (or aggressive) rules on a volume without committing to actually deleting anything.
## New
- **Dry-run mode, per volume.** Each volume in the Volumes tab now has a *Dry run* toggle. When it's on, DotZap evaluates your rules and logs what it *would* have removed — but leaves every file in place. Great for auditing rules on an unfamiliar drive before letting them loose. - **DRY RUN badge in Activity.** Matched-but-not-deleted files appear in the Activity log with a blue **DRY RUN** badge (alongside the existing orange **SKIPPED** badge from the size cap). The tooltip shows which rule matched and notes that nothing was deleted. - **Stats stay honest.** Dry-run entries are visible in Activity but don't count toward your "files deleted" or "bytes freed" totals — so flipping dry-run on a volume won't pollute your lifetime stats.
## Install & update
- **Already installed?** DotZap auto-updates — pick *Check for Updates…* in the About tab to grab it now, or wait for the next scheduled check. - **First time?** Download `DotZap-1.2.1.dmg`, open it, drag DotZap to Applications. First launch: right-click → Open, then System Settings → Privacy & Security → Open Anyway. Then grant Full Disk Access via the banner in the Volumes tab.

item.title: DotZap 1.2.2
item.description:
# DotZap 1.2.2
This release tackles the root cause of those pesky `._*` AppleDouble files that keep coming back on exFAT, FAT32, and NTFS volumes.
## New
- **Strip extended attributes (per volume).** Each volume row in the Volumes tab now has a *Strip extended attributes* action. It walks every file on that volume and clears all extended attributes — Finder color tags, comments, `com.apple.metadata:*`, and friends. macOS regenerates `._*` sidecars on non-APFS volumes whenever a file with xattrs is written, so removing the source xattrs is the durable fix. Sweeping the sidecars only treats the symptom. - **Confirmation before you commit.** The button opens an inline confirmation that spells out exactly what's removed (and warns that some apps — Lightroom, Photos export — store data in xattrs). Cancel or proceed with *Strip Now*. - **Live progress + summary in Activity.** While the strip runs, the volume row shows a spinner. When it finishes, the Activity log gets a purple "system event" entry summarizing how many files were modified (and any errors), so you have a record of the run.
## Install & update
- **Already installed?** DotZap auto-updates — pick *Check for Updates…* in the About tab to grab it now, or wait for the next scheduled check. - **First time?** Download `DotZap-1.2.2.dmg`, open it, drag DotZap to Applications. First launch: right-click → Open, then System Settings → Privacy & Security → Open Anyway. Then grant Full Disk Access via the banner in the Volumes tab.

item.title: DotZap 1.2.3
item.description:
# DotZap 1.2.3
A small quality-of-life release: the Activity log is searchable now.
## New
- **Search in the Activity tab.** A search bar above the event list filters by **filename**, **rule name**, or **volume name** as you type. A clear button (×) resets the query, and an empty-state message tells you when nothing matches. - **Search persists across tabs.** Your search query sticks around when you flip over to Volumes, Rules, or About and back — no re-typing what you were just looking at.
## Install & update
- **Already installed?** DotZap auto-updates — pick *Check for Updates…* in the About tab to grab it now, or wait for the next scheduled check. - **First time?** Download `DotZap-1.2.3.dmg`, open it, drag DotZap to Applications. First launch: right-click → Open, then System Settings → Privacy & Security → Open Anyway. Then grant Full Disk Access via the banner in the Volumes tab.

item.title: DotZap 1.2.4
item.description:
# DotZap 1.2.4
A meatier follow-up to 1.2.0 with three features aimed at giving you more control over what gets deleted, and better tools for finding things after the fact.
## New
- **Dry-run mode, per volume.** Each volume row in the Volumes tab has a *Dry run* toggle. Flip it on and DotZap will evaluate your rules and log what it *would* have removed — without touching a single file. Great for sanity-checking new or aggressive rules on an unfamiliar drive. Matched files appear in Activity with a blue **DRY RUN** badge and a tooltip showing which rule would have caught them. - **Strip extended attributes.** Each volume row also has a *Strip extended attributes* action. It walks every file on the volume and clears all xattrs — Finder color tags, comments, `com.apple.metadata:*`, the works. macOS regenerates `._*` AppleDouble sidecars on non-APFS volumes (exFAT, FAT32, NTFS) every time a file with xattrs is written, so removing the source xattrs is the durable fix; sweeping the sidecars only treats the symptom. The button opens an inline confirmation that spells out exactly what's removed (and warns that some apps — Lightroom, Photos export — store data in xattrs). The volume row shows a spinner while it runs, and a purple system-event row in Activity logs how many files were modified. - **Search the Activity log.** A search bar above the event list filters by **filename**, **rule name**, or **volume name** as you type, with a clear button (×) to reset and an empty-state message when nothing matches. The query persists when you switch tabs, so flipping over to Volumes and back doesn't make you re-type. - **Honest stats.** Dry-run entries (and the existing 1.2.0 size-cap "skipped" entries) are visible in Activity but don't count toward your "files deleted" or "bytes freed" totals.
## Install & update
- **Already installed?** DotZap auto-updates — pick *Check for Updates…* in the About tab to grab it now, or wait for the next scheduled check. - **First time?** Download `DotZap-1.2.4.dmg`, open it, drag DotZap to Applications. First launch: right-click → Open, then System Settings → Privacy & Security → Open Anyway. Then grant Full Disk Access via the banner in the Volumes tab.

item.title: DotZap 1.2.5
item.description:
# DotZap 1.2.5
This release brings DotZap to Shortcuts, Siri, and Spotlight — you can now drive cleaning from automations, voice, or a quick keyboard search.
## New
- **Shortcuts.app integration.** DotZap now ships eight App Intents that show up automatically in Shortcuts.app. You can wire them into automations alongside any other action — for example, run a cleanup whenever a specific drive mounts, or strip xattrs nightly. Intents available: - **Toggle DotZap** / **Pause DotZap** / **Resume DotZap** — global on/off. - **Clean Volume** — one-shot scan on a chosen volume. - **Strip Extended Attributes** — runs the xattr stripper on a chosen volume; returns a summary of how many files were modified. - **Set Volume Enabled** — enable or disable cleaning for a single volume without touching the global state. - **Get DotZap Stats** — returns "*N files cleaned, X freed*" as text you can drop into other actions. - **List DotZap Volumes** — returns the names of currently-mounted, non-ejected volumes. - **Siri & Spotlight phrases.** Three of the most useful actions — *Toggle DotZap*, *Clean Volume*, and *Get Stats* — are also typeable in Spotlight and speakable to Siri without setting up a Shortcut first ("How many files has DotZap cleaned?", "Toggle DotZap", "Clean a volume with DotZap").
## Fixed
- **About tab version display.** The version line now reads "Version 1.2.5" cleanly, without a trailing build number that could be mistaken for a separate version when checking for updates.
## Install & update
- **Already installed?** DotZap auto-updates — pick *Check for Updates…* in the About tab to grab it now, or wait for the next scheduled check. - **First time?** Download `DotZap-1.2.5.dmg`, open it, drag DotZap to Applications. First launch: right-click → Open, then System Settings → Privacy & Security → Open Anyway. Then grant Full Disk Access via the banner in the Volumes tab.

item.title: DotZap 1.2.6
item.description:
# DotZap 1.2.6
A safety release focused on hardening how DotZap decides what to delete, plus a handful of correctness fixes for stats, persistence, and the Full Disk Access banner.
## Fixed
- **Symlinks are now removed as themselves, never followed to their target.** If a path matching a rule was actually a symbolic link (e.g. someone placed `._foo` as a symlink pointing into `~/Documents`), DotZap previously trashed or removed the *target* of the link — which could be anywhere on disk. The fix: symlinks are detected up front and unlinked directly, never resolved. Regular files also get a second containment check that confirms their resolved path is still inside the watched volume before deletion. With Full Disk Access granted, this closes a real footgun on shared or untrusted drives. - **Protected system folders are matched by directory name, not substring.** Paths like `/Volumes/USB/.Spotlight-V100-backup/...` were being incorrectly *skipped*, and a volume mounted at a path containing `/.Spotlight-V100/` would have had its entire contents treated as protected. Now only paths with an exact `.Spotlight-V100`, `.Trashes`, `.fseventsd`, `.TemporaryItems`, or `.DocumentRevisions-V100` directory component are skipped — which is what was meant all along. - **Per-drive stats no longer collide between same-named volumes.** If you had two USB sticks both labeled *Untitled* (or any other shared name — Apple's default for unformatted disks is a common offender), deletions on the second drive were being attributed to the first. Stats now key off the mount path, so each drive's lifetime totals stay independent. - **Full Disk Access banner is more reliable.** The probe used to treat unusual filesystem errors as if FDA were granted, which silently hid the banner on machines that actually didn't have access. It's now strictly conservative — only a successful read counts as granted, so the banner reliably appears whenever access is missing. - **Persistence errors no longer silently wipe saved state.** If DotZap can't decode its stored rules, volumes, or whitelists (corrupted preferences, partial write, schema mismatch), it now logs the failure to the system console and keeps the original bad blob under a `.corrupted-<timestamp>` key — rather than resetting to an empty list with no trace.
## Changed
- **The custom-rule editor catches a confusing pattern.** When the **Prefix** match type is selected with a `*` in the pattern, the form now shows an inline warning and disables Save. Prefix is a literal *starts-with* check, so `foo*bar` doesn't mean what it would in **Glob** mode. Any existing rules in this state — including the built-in **Apple Double** rule (`._*`) — are automatically promoted to **Glob** on first launch so they keep matching what you intended. - **Ejected drives get pruned whenever any drive disappears**, not only at app launch. Long-running installs that never quit will no longer accumulate stale Volumes-tab entries for drives that haven't been seen in over a week. - **Tightened the bundled entitlements.** Removed a sandbox-only entitlement that wasn't doing anything (DotZap runs outside the sandbox by design so it can watch every mount). No behavior change.
## Install & update
- **Already installed?** DotZap auto-updates — pick *Check for Updates…* in the About tab to grab it now, or wait for the next scheduled check. - **First time?** Download `DotZap-1.2.6.dmg`, open it, drag DotZap to Applications. First launch: right-click → Open, then System Settings → Privacy & Security → Open Anyway. Then grant Full Disk Access via the banner in the Volumes tab.
